# Questions in Livestream 33

Live Stream #33: Twenty-Twenty Too

Time links open the YouTube video at the relevant timestamp. The links use HTML anchors so GitHub can render them with `target="_blank"`.

| Time | Question | Short answer / answer direction |
|---:|---|---|
| <a href="https://youtu.be/pc-P4s6IR2o?t=317" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">5:17</a> | What did ancient Egyptian pharaohs and viziers wear? | He said pharaohs were typically shown in a kilt and headdress, while viziers had a much more distinctive long robe-like garment. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/pc-P4s6IR2o?t=521" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">8:41</a> | What is his view of icons in worship? | He said Eastern Christian icon use is nuanced and not simply idolatry, but he remains personally cautious about the practice. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/pc-P4s6IR2o?t=792" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">13:12</a> | In light of Dr. Kipp's article, will he respond, and which biblical scholars should people read on the topic? | He gave only a joking hint about a future response, then recommended scholars such as Emanuel Tov while urging people to read widely and keep evaluating arguments. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/pc-P4s6IR2o?t=1015" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">16:55</a> | Are there non-religious Egyptologists who still believe in some kind of Exodus? | Yes. He mentioned positions ranging from memory-based models to scholars such as Manfred Bietak who take an Exodus more seriously without being religious apologists. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/pc-P4s6IR2o?t=1207" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">20:07</a> | Is there good scriptural basis for understanding the conquest as a long process rather than a single short campaign? | He said yes. In his reading, Joshua breaks the major centers first, then the tribes spend decades securing their own inheritances into the judges period. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/pc-P4s6IR2o?t=1430" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">23:50</a> | Does he currently lean toward a global or local flood, and does he agree with Answers in Genesis on the flood? | He said he was still working through it and leaned global, but he had major problems with Answers in Genesis' chronology, exegesis, and reasoning. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/pc-P4s6IR2o?t=1622" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">27:02</a> | Has he seen Truthunedited's video on the history of religion, and does he agree with its claims about paganism, polytheism, Nimrod, Semiramis, and Tammuz? | He had not seen the video, so he did not judge its claims, but he said Nimrod may have been historical, Semiramis had a real Assyrian basis later mythologized, and Tammuz is not a resurrection myth. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/pc-P4s6IR2o?t=1884" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">31:24</a> | Does the United Nations peace-and-safety statue resemble the beast of Revelation 13, and could its imagery mark the end? | He said no. Apart from a superficial leopard-like feature, he did not think it matched Revelation, and he said Christians have been in the end days since the ascension rather than using the statue as a date marker. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/pc-P4s6IR2o?t=2177" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">36:17</a> | Why has a strong candidate for Sodom and Gomorrah not been found by the Dead Sea? | He said archaeology often leaves major sites undiscovered, so the absence of a good candidate is frustrating but not surprising. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/pc-P4s6IR2o?t=2431" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">40:31</a> | What is the onomasticon problem? | He explained it as the risk that later remembered or recorded place names may not preserve the true ancient locations, especially when settlements, roads, regions, and names shift over time. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/pc-P4s6IR2o?t=2679" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">44:39</a> | Does Deuteronomy 2:8 imply that the Arabah south of the Dead Sea was called the Jordan Valley, changing where Sodom should be placed? | He agreed that Sodom and Gomorrah likely belong south of the Dead Sea and said northern Dead Sea sites may still be archaeologically valuable but are probably not Sodom. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/pc-P4s6IR2o?t=2767" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">46:07</a> | Why are mentions of Sodom and Gomorrah not found in contemporary records such as Ebla? | He said Ebla's records are still difficult and incomplete, its sphere of interest likely did not reach that area, and Egyptian records from the relevant period should not be expected to mention the cities either. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/pc-P4s6IR2o?t=2979" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">49:39</a> | What are his thoughts on Kara Cooney's Egyptology work and comments connected to Kyle Rittenhouse? | He said he knows her and disagrees with her recent activist framing, especially when modern politics are anachronistically applied to ancient Egypt or used irresponsibly. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/pc-P4s6IR2o?t=3128" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">52:08</a> | How would he answer claims that Exodus is a late fictional borrowing from Gilgamesh or that Gerald Massey debunked Christianity? | He dismissed those claims as implausible, noting that Amos already refers to God bringing Israel out of Egypt, which does not fit a first-century-BC invention. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/pc-P4s6IR2o?t=3318" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">55:18</a> | Did the Egyptians believe in evolution? | No. He said Egyptian mythology has multiple special-creation stories, but evolution is a modern European idea and not part of Egyptian mythology. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/pc-P4s6IR2o?t=3418" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">56:58</a> | What would he say to scholars who think the conquest was genocidal? | He rejected the genocide framing, saying the conquest was about driving people out and occupying cities; he later remembered John Walton as the scholar whose reading of herem he would side with. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/pc-P4s6IR2o?t=3654" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:00:54</a> | How accurate is it to say Copts are direct descendants of ancient Egyptians compared with recent DNA research? | He said there is a case for Copts as descendants of ancient Egyptians, but modern Egyptian ancestry varies regionally and includes several later population contributions. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/pc-P4s6IR2o?t=4098" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:08:18</a> | What are his thoughts on the identity of the Amalekites, since they do not seem to appear in extrabiblical texts? | He described them as a likely semi-nomadic regional group in and around Sinai, hard to trace directly in texts, but not therefore doubtful as a historical people. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/pc-P4s6IR2o?t=4337" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:12:17</a> | Would he review David Rohl's *Egyptian Genesis* documentary or comment on Rohl's alleged Egyptian paradise island in the Persian Gulf? | He said he had already done substantial material on Rohl, did not know that specific paradise-island claim, and might only review more if usable material and copyright constraints allowed it. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/pc-P4s6IR2o?t=4589" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:16:29</a> | Can versions of the Documentary Hypothesis fit with inspiration, such as Moses writing one source and later followers writing another? | He said some modified versions could fit inspiration, but the traditional late J-source framework is much harder to reconcile and he rejects the hypothesis mainly on methodological and factual grounds. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/pc-P4s6IR2o?t=4932" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:22:12</a> | Which pharaoh was Solomon's father-in-law? | He said Siamun is the most likely candidate, with Psusennes II also possible, and argued that Egypt's weak Dynasty 21 context makes such a treaty marriage credible. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/pc-P4s6IR2o?t=5207" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:26:47</a> | What is the context and meaning of recruiting Abishag the Shunammite to keep elderly King David warm? | He framed it within ancient Near Eastern sexual and court politics: she could serve David's comfort, but her association with the royal harem also created succession implications. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/pc-P4s6IR2o?t=5465" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:31:05</a> | What is going on at Tanis, and are archaeologists finding interesting material there? | He said active excavations were finding material, including areas around granaries and royal tombs, but publication was slow. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/pc-P4s6IR2o?t=5600" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:33:20</a> | Were there any new discoveries in 2021, and might 2022 bring more? | He mentioned strong 2021 discoveries including Old Kingdom material, Saqqara tomb work, and an Apries stela, while saying pandemic disruption had slowed excavation and publication. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/pc-P4s6IR2o?t=5783" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:36:23</a> | Will he do movie reviews of films such as *Stargate*, *The Ten Commandments*, *Cleopatra*, or *The Mummy Returns*? | He said he would like to review films, especially historically bad Egypt-themed ones, but copyright enforcement makes them difficult and risky. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/pc-P4s6IR2o?t=6130" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:42:10</a> | What books would he recommend on ancient Near Eastern literary patterns? | He recommended John Walton's "Lost World" books and especially Robert Alter on biblical poetry and narrative. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/pc-P4s6IR2o?t=6331" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:45:31</a> | How did ancient Egyptians brush their teeth, if they did? | He said they generally did not brush their teeth, apart from a much later Hellenistic toothbrush, and that diet, especially dates, contributed to tooth decay. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/pc-P4s6IR2o?t=6429" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:47:09</a> | Did Egyptians shave all their body hair? | He said yes, especially for lice control and ritual cleanliness, with wigs, false beards, and children's sidelocks as important exceptions. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/pc-P4s6IR2o?t=6555" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:49:15</a> | Did biblical writers have a geocentric flat-earth view of the cosmos, and is that a problem for Christians? | He described it as more of a sliced cosmology than flat earth and said it is not a problem if read as analogical, literary language rather than rigid literal description. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/pc-P4s6IR2o?t=6753" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:52:33</a> | Are there ancient garbage dump sites that need to be excavated in Egypt and Israel? | He said probably, stressing that garbage is central to archaeology and pointing to examples such as Oxyrhynchus, Deir el-Medina ostraca, and sifted Temple Mount debris. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/pc-P4s6IR2o?t=7087" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:58:07</a> | What are his thoughts on Tremper Longman's *Confronting Old Testament Controversies*? | He had not read the book, but said he generally likes Longman's work and regards him as reliable in other things. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/pc-P4s6IR2o?t=7259" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:00:59</a> | What are his thoughts on the Third Intermediate Period and why Egypt never recovered New Kingdom-level power and influence? | He said Egypt's division between competing rulers left it vulnerable to foreign powers, and later invasions and occupations prevented a return to New Kingdom strength. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/pc-P4s6IR2o?t=7531" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:05:31</a> | How does he take notes when reading? | He underlines key passages, then uses the blank front pages of the book as a compact index with page numbers and brief topic labels, while noting that different systems suit different learners. |
